meta.title: MagonOS — standalone product layer
meta.description: MagonOS brings company intelligence, commercial prep, and operator workflow together in one standalone web layer.

header.mobileMenuText: Switch language, adjust the theme, and jump into the working surfaces from here.

appearance.title: Interface palettes
appearance.description: Palettes change the accent and glow tokens without disturbing page structure or component hierarchy.

ember-signal.name: Cobalt contour
ember-signal.description: Default paper-led palette with a clear route accent

tidal-mint.name: Signal cyan
tidal-mint.description: Lighter mode for trust signals and verification work

violet-ink.name: Industrial red
violet-ink.description: A stricter mode for risk, tolerance and manual control

page.title: The line between the public shell, the standalone core, and the remaining ERP tails should be obvious at a glance.
page.text: This page states the current product contract. Some things are already owned by standalone, some still run in bridge mode, and the UI should not blur that distinction.
page.contractText: The public shell and the operator workflow should behave like one product layer. Internal ERP links are acceptable only as a transition dependency, not as the place where new logic should grow.

rules.one: If an entity is part of the product long term, it needs a standalone source of truth instead of living only in an external historical contour.
rules.two: The public shell should explain the working product contour clearly: company intelligence, commercial context, handoff prep, and operator workflow.
rules.three: Bridge dependencies must be shown honestly. A transitional contour should not be presented as a completed migration.
rules.four: New work belongs in the standalone repository by default. Historical source code is for inspection, extraction, or final cut-off only.

home.title: MagonOS moves a company from canonical intake and operator review into commercial context and production handoff.
home.text: This is no longer a shell sitting on top of an old external contour. The active contour now lives in standalone: company intelligence, review, customer context, opportunity tracking, and quote intent. Internal ERP links still exist, but they no longer define the product layer.

highlights.one: Company intelligence is stored in standalone and does not depend on a legacy back-office runtime.
highlights.two: Review, qualification, and routing feed the same company-centric operator workflow.
highlights.three: Customer accounts, opportunities, and quote intent now have their own product boundary instead of living as external references only.
highlights.four: Production handoff and the production board sit in the same runtime as the company workbench.

home.focusTitle: The active workflow is already standalone-owned.
home.focusText: Company intelligence, review, commercial context, and handoff prep live here. Full ERP and CRM parity is still unfinished, so bridge dependencies still need explicit tracking.
home.runtimeOffline: The backend is unavailable right now. The public shell is up, but the operator contour did not respond.
home.surfacesTitle: This side of the screen should make the next action obvious.
home.surfacesText: Instead of a dead decorative panel, this area should show where to inspect the system, where to run operations, and where to read the project contour.

dashboard.title: Runtime
dashboard.body: Open the current standalone summary, data counts, and working surfaces.

ops.title: Ops
ops.body: Go straight into companies, review, feedback, and production-prep work.

map.title: Project map
map.body: See what standalone owns, where overlap still exists, and which automations keep the repo in sync.

flowPreview.one: Start with the current runtime state and the company data already inside standalone.
flowPreview.two: Then the operator moves through review, commercial context, and quote preparation.
flowPreview.three: After that, the work moves into handoff and production control without splitting into a separate external surface.

standalone.title: What is already native
standalone.body: PostgreSQL, canonical companies, review, routing, commercial prep, and handoff preparation already belong to standalone.

bridge.title: Where the bridge remains
bridge.body: Some CRM and ERP semantics, plus historical identifiers, still carry transition-era traces. That is a temporary fact, not the product target.

next.title: What comes next
next.body: The next step is to cut off the remaining commercial and ERP tails so standalone becomes the only product-of-record.

home.operatorTitle: The working surfaces now live in the same product as the public shell.
home.operatorText: From here you open companies, the review queue, feedback status, customer context, quote intent, and production prep. A separate table-first site is no longer the main entry point.

dashboard.title: What already lives in standalone and is available to the operator today.
dashboard.text: This view shows the current standalone runtime: data volume, working surfaces, and quick entry points into the actual operator flow. It is neither a marketing shell nor a context-free debug page.
dashboard.runtimeUnavailable: The backend did not respond. Start the standalone runtime before opening the operator surfaces.
dashboard.workspaceTitle: Where the operator enters from the shared product shell
dashboard.workspaceText: These are direct entry points into the current standalone-first contour: suppliers, intake requests, and orders. All three now live inside the same product shell as the public layer.

suppliers.title: Suppliers
suppliers.body: Open the supplier registry, trust levels, ingest jobs, and manual verification in one place.

requests.title: Requests
requests.body: Go into the intake workbench, `draft/request` states, and operator transitions without the legacy review queue.

orders.title: Orders
orders.body: Open the order workbench, documents, and confirmed execution without the legacy production board.

dashboard.recentEmpty: There are no companies to show in the runtime yet.

opsWorkbench.title: The operator layer now sits inside the same standalone product and leads into the real working paths.
opsWorkbench.text: This page connects the public shell to the active runtime surfaces. There is no legacy runtime here and no decorative imitation of an operator UI.
opsWorkbench.runtimeUnavailable: Start the backend runtime before entering the operator surfaces.
opsWorkbench.surfacesTitle: Where the operator goes from the unified product
opsWorkbench.surfacesText: These are active entry points into the current standalone core. They lead into working screens, not decorative placeholders.

companies.title: Company workbench
companies.body: Open supplier and company intelligence, scores, review, feedback, and commercial linkage.

review.title: Review queue
review.body: Open the review queue and continue into the company-centric decision flow.

feedback.title: Feedback status
feedback.body: Inspect downstream outcomes separately from canonical intelligence.

board.title: Production board
board.body: Open production-prep and handoff execution surfaces.

opsWorkbench.recentText: The latest companies available for operator drill-down.
opsWorkbench.recentEmpty: No companies have been loaded yet.
opsWorkbench.bridgeTitle: The public shell and the operator surfaces now live in one standalone repository.
opsWorkbench.bridgeText: The app root can now remain a product-facing shell while the ops workbench sits beside it as an embedded working layer. That is cleaner than replacing the product surface with backend tables.

projectMap.title: The project map shows the working contour, risks, and automated checks in one place.
projectMap.text: This surface should read as a live map of the product: what standalone already owns, where dangerous overlap still remains, and which checks keep the system aligned.
projectMap.fallbackFocus: No active focus has been extracted yet
projectMap.fallbackRisk: The operational risk has not been extracted from project memory yet.
projectMap.flowTitle: How the working chain moves today
projectMap.automationTitle: Which recurring checks should keep the project aligned
projectMap.recentTitle: What changed recently in the project

referencePage.title: This page gathers the key entities, dependencies, and working paths of wave1 without guesswork or architecture theater.
referencePage.text: Use this as the fastest entry into the current project context: what an entity is, what it depends on, and which screen actually owns it. It does not replace the wave1 spec; it helps you orient inside the standalone contour that already exists.
referencePage.quickTitle: Find the entity first, then the screen, then the dependency boundary.
referencePage.quickText: If the ownership is unclear, use this rule: the customer starts in the public shell and draft/request view, the operator works in request/order/supplier surfaces, and the admin inspects dashboards, audit, and the project map. Anything outside wave1 is explicitly called out at the bottom.
referencePage.flowTitle: The product flow that should already be readable in the interface and in the data model
referencePage.flowText: Do not collapse these entities into one generic record. They are deliberately split so transitions, reasons, audit, and role-scoped visibility do not turn into one muddy object.
referencePage.entitiesTitle: What these objects are and how to use them in the project
referencePage.depsTitle: What depends on what and where the boundary must stay explicit
referencePage.rolesTitle: Which screens and routes belong to which role
referencePage.limitsTitle: Intentionally left outside the current contour

marketingPage.title: Wave1 marketing should lead into clear intake, not hide the work behind a decorative landing page.
marketingPage.text: The spec defines a light showcase, guest draft intake, and a separate complex RFQ entry. This page turns that into one public marketing surface: promise, entry points, and the path from interest to the actual request contour.
marketingPage.promiseTitle: The public layer must stay commercially clear.
marketingPage.promiseText: Instead of vague AI claims and ERP jargon, this layer needs readable directions, honest pricing language, and a fast path into manual review.

promises.one: A constrained wave1 showcase instead of pretending to be a marketplace for everything.
promises.two: Guest draft intake for straightforward cases without forced registration.
promises.three: A separate RFQ entry for complex and non-standard requests.

marketingPage.flowTitle: The marketing entry should end in a real working contour.

one.title: Showcase and commercial directions
one.body: The visitor sees limited product directions, indicative pricing, and clear calls to action without noise.

two.title: Draft or RFQ
two.body: A simple case goes into a guest draft, a complex one goes into the dedicated RFQ route. These entry points stay intentionally separate.

three.title: Request and offer contour
three.body: After required fields are checked, the operator moves the draft into a request, prepares versioned offers, and keeps the full decision history.

marketingPage.surfacesTitle: The entry points need to explain the difference between a simple and a complex path.

catalog.title: Showcase
catalog.body: Displays the curated wave1 directions, product cards, and the path into a draft.

rfq.title: Complex RFQ
rfq.body: A dedicated entry for custom work where a product card cannot honestly promise the final price.

request.title: Operator review contour
request.body: After submit, the operator enters the request/offer/order contour through login and continues the case without mixing public and internal surfaces.

catalogShowcase.title: Show clear directions and a constrained set of products instead of pretending to be a marketplace for everything.
catalogShowcase.text: This showcase exists for guest draft/RFQ intake. It contains only a curated wave1 set: understandable cards, indicative pricing, and a direct path into a request without forced registration.

catalogDetail.priceOnRequest: Pricing is confirmed only after manual estimation
catalogDetail.draftSummary: We need a fast estimate for this card. Please confirm size, material, volume, and deadlines.
catalogDetail.rfqSummary: We need a custom project with manual review. Describe the product, volume, deadlines, materials, and constraints.

catalogForms.fullTitle: Send a complex request into manual review
catalogForms.linkedItem: The context is already tied to this card: {title}
catalogForms.generalLead: Submit as a guest. An operator will review the input and move it into the request contour without forcing registration.
catalogForms.title: Request title
catalogForms.contextPrefill: Need manual review and estimation for: {title}
catalogForms.contextPlaceholder: Briefly describe the item or service around which this request is built.
catalogForms.summaryPlaceholder: Describe the product, size, material, volume, deadlines, and constraints.
catalogForms.websiteTrap: If you are a bot, fill this field
catalogForms.privacyHint: No registration required. This is a manual-first wave1 intake entry point.
catalogForms.success: Draft created: {code}. The standalone draft editor with autosave and submit guards will open next.

rfqPage.title: RFQ stays a separate entry point when a product card cannot honestly promise a final price.
rfqPage.text: If the project is non-standard, we do not fake a calculator. The request goes into manual intake so the operator can review context, constraints, and feasibility before moving it into request/offer.
rfqPage.defaultSummary: Describe the product, task, volume, deadlines, materials, production constraints, and what matters most for evaluation.
